max_of_t.name: Max-Of-t Test
max_of_t.nist_reference: P. L'Ecuyer and R. Simard, TestU01: A C Library for Empirical Testing of Random Number Generators, ACM TOMACS, 2007
max_of_t.summary: The Max-Of-t test generates groups of t uniform random values, records the maximum of each group, and tests whether the maxima distribution matches the expected power-law CDF.
max_of_t.description:
This test generates n groups of t U(0,1) values (default t=5, n=10000) and records the maximum value from each group. Under the null hypothesis, the maximum of t independent uniform values has CDF F(x) = x^t.

The observed distribution of maxima is compared against the theoretical distribution using a chi-squared goodness-of-fit test. The [0,1] interval is divided into d equal bins, with expected counts computed from the power-law CDF.

This test is sensitive to generators whose extreme-value behavior deviates from theory, which can happen when the generator has subtle correlations between consecutive values or truncation artifacts. This is Crush test #48-51.
max_of_t.mathematical_basis: Generate n=10000 groups of t=5 U(0,1) values. Max M = max(U_1,...,U_t). CDF: F(x) = x^5. Divide [0,1] into d=20 bins. E_i = n * ((i+1)/d)^t - (i/d)^t). Chi-squared with 19 df. P-value = cephes_igamc(19/2.0, chi2/2.0).

parameters.bitstream_count: Number of independent bitstreams to test (default: 1)
parameters.bitstream_length: Length of each bitstream in bits (minimum: 1600000 bits)

interpretation.pass: The distribution of group maxima matches the expected x^t power-law CDF.
interpretation.fail: Maxima deviate from the expected distribution, indicating correlation or distributional defects.

max_of_t.recommendations: Requires at least 1,600,000 bits. This test probes extreme-value behavior which is critical for Monte Carlo simulations and risk analysis applications.
example_applications: Testing extreme-value properties of random number generators
Evaluating generators for Monte Carlo risk analysis
Detecting correlation artifacts in group statistics

run.name: Run Test
run.nist_reference: P. L'Ecuyer and R. Simard, TestU01: A C Library for Empirical Testing of Random Number Generators, ACM TOMACS, 2007
run.summary: The Run test counts maximal subsequences of consecutive increasing values (runs up) and compares the distribution of run lengths against the theoretical expectation using a chi-squared test.
run.description:
This test (sknuth_Run in TestU01) converts the bitstream to a sequence of uniform [0,1) values and identifies 'runs up' — maximal subsequences where each value is larger than the previous one. When a value is smaller than its predecessor, a run ends and a new one begins.

The number of runs of each length (1, 2, 3, 4, 5, and >=6) are tallied. Under the null hypothesis of independence, the probability that a run has length exactly k is 1/k! - 1/(k+1)!, and the probability of a run of length >= 6 is 1/6!.

A chi-squared goodness-of-fit test with 5 degrees of freedom compares observed run counts to these theoretical probabilities. This corresponds to Crush tests #48-49.
run.mathematical_basis: Convert bitstream to n uniform values. Count runs up of lengths 1..5 and >=6. P(length=k) = 1/k! - 1/(k+1)! for k=1..5; P(length>=6) = 1/6!. Chi-squared test with 5 df. P-value = igamc(5/2, chi2/2).

parameters.bitstream_count: Number of independent bitstreams to test (default: 1)
parameters.bitstream_length: Length of each bitstream in bits (minimum: 8192 bits)

interpretation.pass: The distribution of run lengths matches the expected theoretical distribution, consistent with independence.
interpretation.fail: Run lengths deviate significantly from expectation, indicating trends or dependencies in consecutive outputs.

run.recommendations: Use at least 1,000,000 bits for meaningful results. This test is particularly effective at detecting generators that produce overly smooth or overly jagged sequences of consecutive values.
example_applications: Detecting monotone trends in random number generators
Testing independence of consecutive generator outputs
Quality assurance for Monte Carlo simulation inputs

permutation.name: Permutation Test
permutation.nist_reference: P. L'Ecuyer and R. Simard, TestU01: A C Library for Empirical Testing of Random Number Generators, ACM TOMACS, 2007
permutation.summary: The Permutation test examines non-overlapping t-tuples of uniform values, determines the ordering permutation of each tuple, and tests whether all t! permutations occur with equal frequency.
permutation.description:
This test (sknuth_Permutation in TestU01) converts the bitstream to a sequence of uniform [0,1) values and takes non-overlapping tuples of t consecutive values (default t=5). For each tuple, it determines the rank of the permutation that describes the ordering of the elements using the Lehmer code.

Under the null hypothesis of independence, each of the t! = 120 permutations should be equally likely. The observed frequency of each permutation is compared against the expected uniform distribution using a chi-squared test with t! - 1 = 119 degrees of freedom.

This test is highly effective at detecting subtle dependencies between consecutive values that preserve marginal uniformity but distort ordering structure. This corresponds to Crush tests #50-51.
permutation.mathematical_basis: Take non-overlapping 5-tuples from uniform sequence. Compute Lehmer code rank in [0, 120). Expected count = N/120 per permutation. Chi-squared with 119 df. P-value = igamc(119/2, chi2/2).

parameters.bitstream_count: Number of independent bitstreams to test (default: 1)
parameters.bitstream_length: Length of each bitstream in bits (minimum: 8192 bits)

interpretation.pass: All permutations of t-tuples occur with approximately equal frequency, consistent with independence.
interpretation.fail: Certain permutations are over- or under-represented, indicating dependencies in the ordering of consecutive values.

permutation.recommendations: Use at least 1,000,000 bits to ensure sufficient samples per permutation category. With t=5 and 120 categories, at least ~12,000 tuples (1,920,000 bits at 32 bits/value) are recommended.
example_applications: Detecting ordering dependencies in random number generators
Testing for subtle correlation structures
Evaluating generators for simulation applications requiring independence

sample_product.name: Sample Product Test
sample_product.nist_reference: P. L'Ecuyer and R. Simard, TestU01: A C Library for Empirical Testing of Random Number Generators, ACM TOMACS, 2007
sample_product.summary: The Sample Product test computes products of groups of uniform random values and tests whether the transformed products follow the expected chi-squared distribution.
sample_product.description:
This test (svaria_SampleProd in TestU01) generates groups of t uniform [0,1) values (default t=10) and computes the product of each group. Under the null hypothesis, if U_1,...,U_t are independent uniform, then -2*ln(U_1*...*U_t) follows a chi-squared distribution with 2t degrees of freedom.

The test transforms each product using this relationship to obtain values that should follow chi-squared(2t), then converts these to uniform p-values via the chi-squared CDF. An Anderson-Darling goodness-of-fit test is applied to these p-values to assess uniformity.

This test is sensitive to correlations in the multiplicative structure of the generator — it can detect cases where products of consecutive values are systematically too large or too small. This corresponds to Crush tests #57-58.
sample_product.mathematical_basis: Groups of t=10 values, product P = prod(U_i). Transform: Y = -2*ln(P) ~ chi2(2t). Convert to uniform via CDF. Anderson-Darling test on resulting p-values with Marsaglia-Marsaglia approximation for AD p-value.

parameters.bitstream_count: Number of independent bitstreams to test (default: 1)
parameters.bitstream_length: Length of each bitstream in bits (minimum: 8192 bits)

interpretation.pass: Products of uniform groups follow the expected distribution, consistent with independence.
interpretation.fail: Product distributions deviate from theory, indicating multiplicative correlation artifacts.

sample_product.recommendations: Use at least 1,000,000 bits. This test probes a different aspect of dependence than run or permutation tests — it is sensitive to generators where the logarithmic structure of consecutive values is flawed.
example_applications: Testing multiplicative independence of random number generators
Evaluating generators for applications involving product statistics
Detecting logarithmic correlation patterns

sample_mean.name: Sample Mean Test
sample_mean.nist_reference: P. L'Ecuyer and R. Simard, TestU01: A C Library for Empirical Testing of Random Number Generators, ACM TOMACS, 2007
sample_mean.summary: The Sample Mean test computes the mean of groups of uniform random values and tests whether these means follow the expected normal distribution via the Central Limit Theorem.
sample_mean.description:
This test (svaria_SampleMean in TestU01) generates groups of n uniform [0,1) values (default n=30) and computes the sample mean of each group. By the Central Limit Theorem, these means are approximately normally distributed with mean 0.5 and variance 1/(12n).

Each sample mean is standardized to a z-score, then converted to a uniform p-value via the standard normal CDF. An Anderson-Darling goodness-of-fit test is applied to these p-values.

This test detects generators where the first moment (mean) of groups of consecutive values deviates from theoretical expectations, which can occur with generators that have subtle biases or drift in their output distribution. This corresponds to Crush test #59.
sample_mean.mathematical_basis: Groups of n=30 values, mean M_bar ~ N(0.5, 1/(12*30)). Standardize: z = (M_bar - 0.5) / sqrt(1/360). Convert to uniform via Phi(z). Anderson-Darling test on p-values.

parameters.bitstream_count: Number of independent bitstreams to test (default: 1)
parameters.bitstream_length: Length of each bitstream in bits (minimum: 8192 bits)

interpretation.pass: Sample means follow the expected normal distribution, consistent with unbiased uniform generation.
interpretation.fail: Sample means deviate from the expected distribution, indicating bias or drift in the generator.

sample_mean.recommendations: Use at least 1,000,000 bits. With n=30 per group and 32 bits per value, this yields ~1040 groups from 1M bits, which is sufficient for the Anderson-Darling test.
example_applications: Detecting mean bias in random number generators
Testing first-moment properties of generator output
Evaluating generators for statistical simulation accuracy

sample_correlation.name: Sample Correlation Test
sample_correlation.nist_reference: P. L'Ecuyer and R. Simard, TestU01: A C Library for Empirical Testing of Random Number Generators, ACM TOMACS, 2007
sample_correlation.summary: The Sample Correlation test computes the lag-1 autocorrelation of a sequence of uniform values and tests whether it is consistent with zero (indicating independence).
sample_correlation.description:
This test (svaria_SampleCorr in TestU01) generates a sequence of uniform [0,1) values from the bitstream and computes the lag-1 sample autocorrelation coefficient r = Cov(U_i, U_{i+1}) / Var(U_i).

Under the null hypothesis of independence, this autocorrelation should be approximately zero with variance 1/n, where n is the number of values. The test standardizes the autocorrelation to a z-score (z = r * sqrt(n)) and computes a two-sided p-value from the standard normal distribution.

This is one of the most direct tests for serial dependence — it specifically targets linear correlations between consecutive outputs. Even generators that pass frequency and gap tests can fail this test if they have subtle linear dependencies. This corresponds to Crush test #60.
sample_correlation.mathematical_basis: Compute lag-1 autocorrelation r = (1/(n-1)) * sum((U_i - mean)(U_{i+1} - mean)) / var. Under H0: r ~ N(0, 1/n). z = r*sqrt(n). Two-sided p-value = erfc(|z|/sqrt(2)).

parameters.bitstream_count: Number of independent bitstreams to test (default: 1)
parameters.bitstream_length: Length of each bitstream in bits (minimum: 8192 bits)

interpretation.pass: The autocorrelation is consistent with zero, indicating no detectable serial dependence.
interpretation.fail: Significant autocorrelation detected, indicating linear dependence between consecutive outputs.

sample_correlation.recommendations: Use at least 1,000,000 bits for best sensitivity. This test is a fundamental check for serial independence and should be included in any comprehensive randomness evaluation.
example_applications: Detecting serial dependence in random number generators
Testing for lag-1 linear correlation in generator output
Evaluating generators for applications requiring statistical independence

random_walk.name: Random Walk Test
random_walk.nist_reference: P. L'Ecuyer and R. Simard, TestU01: A C Library for Empirical Testing of Random Number Generators, ACM TOMACS, 2007
random_walk.summary: The Random Walk test maps bits to +1/-1 steps, performs multiple walks, and tests whether the distribution of final positions matches the expected normal distribution.
random_walk.description:
This test (swalk_RandomWalk1 in TestU01) converts the bitstream to uniform [0,1) values and maps each to +1 (if >= 0.5) or -1 (if < 0.5). Multiple random walks of a given length are performed. The final position S_n of each walk is normalized by sqrt(n) to give a z-score, which should follow a standard normal distribution under H0.

The z-scores are binned into 6 equal-probability categories based on normal quantiles, and a chi-squared goodness-of-fit test with 5 degrees of freedom is applied. This test detects generators that produce biased or overly regular walk trajectories, including subtle dependencies that affect cumulative sums.
random_walk.mathematical_basis: Each walk: S_n = sum of +1/-1 steps. z = S_n/sqrt(n) ~ N(0,1) under H0. Bin z-scores into 6 equal-probability bins. Chi-squared with df=5. P-value = igamc(5/2, chi2/2).

parameters.bitstream_count: Number of independent bitstreams to test (default: 1)
parameters.bitstream_length: Length of each bitstream in bits (minimum: 8192 bits)

interpretation.pass: Walk final positions match the expected normal distribution, consistent with randomness.
interpretation.fail: Walk endpoints deviate from the normal distribution, indicating bias or dependencies in cumulative sums.

random_walk.recommendations: Use at least 1,000,000 bits with walk length 1000 for sufficient walks (~31 walks). Longer bitstreams yield more walks and better statistical power.
example_applications: Detecting cumulative bias in random number generators
Testing for subtle serial dependencies via walk statistics
Quality assurance for Monte Carlo random walk simulations

hamming_weight.name: Hamming Weight Test
hamming_weight.nist_reference: P. L'Ecuyer and R. Simard, TestU01: A C Library for Empirical Testing of Random Number Generators, ACM TOMACS, 2007
hamming_weight.summary: The Hamming Weight test divides the bitstream into blocks, counts the number of 1-bits per block, and tests whether the weight distribution matches the expected binomial distribution.
hamming_weight.description:
This test (sstring_HammingWeight in TestU01) divides the bitstream into non-overlapping blocks of a given size and counts the Hamming weight (number of 1-bits) of each block. Under H0, the weight follows a Binomial(blockSize, 0.5) distribution, which is approximately normal for large blocks.

The weights are binned into 6 categories based on deviations from the mean (using sigma boundaries), and a chi-squared goodness-of-fit test is applied. This test detects generators with biased bit patterns or non-uniform weight distributions in output words.
hamming_weight.mathematical_basis: Weight W ~ Binomial(L, 0.5) with mean L/2 and sigma sqrt(L/4). Bin weights into 6 categories around the mean. Chi-squared with df=5. P-value = igamc(5/2, chi2/2).

parameters.bitstream_count: Number of independent bitstreams to test (default: 1)
parameters.bitstream_length: Length of each bitstream in bits (minimum: 8192 bits)
parameters.block_size: Size of each block in bits (default: 32)

interpretation.pass: Block Hamming weights follow the expected binomial distribution, consistent with randomness.
interpretation.fail: Weight distribution deviates significantly, indicating biased bit patterns in output blocks.

hamming_weight.recommendations: Use at least 1,000,000 bits with block size 32. This test is particularly effective at detecting generators with biased individual output words.
example_applications: Detecting weight bias in generator output words
Testing for non-uniform bit distributions within blocks
Evaluating hardware random number generators for bit-level balance

hamming_correlation.name: Hamming Correlation Test
hamming_correlation.nist_reference: P. L'Ecuyer and R. Simard, TestU01: A C Library for Empirical Testing of Random Number Generators, ACM TOMACS, 2007
hamming_correlation.summary: The Hamming Correlation test measures the lag-1 autocorrelation between the Hamming weights of consecutive output blocks.
hamming_correlation.description:
This test (sstring_HammingCorr in TestU01) divides the bitstream into non-overlapping blocks and computes the Hamming weight of each block. It then measures the lag-1 autocorrelation between consecutive block weights.

Under H0, the correlation coefficient should be approximately zero. The test standardizes the sample correlation to a z-score and computes a two-sided p-value. It detects generators that produce serial dependencies in the weight structure of consecutive output words — a subtle defect that can affect applications depending on the independence of successive output values.
hamming_correlation.mathematical_basis: Compute Hamming weights w_i for each block. r = Cov(w_i, w_{i+1}) / Var(w). z = r*sqrt(numBlocks). Two-sided p-value = erfc(|z|/sqrt(2)).

parameters.bitstream_count: Number of independent bitstreams to test (default: 1)
parameters.bitstream_length: Length of each bitstream in bits (minimum: 8192 bits)
parameters.block_size: Size of each block in bits (default: 32)

interpretation.pass: No significant correlation between consecutive block weights, consistent with independence.
interpretation.fail: Significant weight correlation detected, indicating dependencies between consecutive output words.

hamming_correlation.recommendations: Use at least 1,000,000 bits. This test complements the Hamming Weight test by checking for temporal dependencies rather than distributional bias.
example_applications: Detecting serial dependencies in generator output word weights
Testing for correlations in hardware random number generators
Evaluating generators for cryptographic applications requiring independence

hamming_independence.name: Hamming Independence Test
hamming_independence.nist_reference: P. L'Ecuyer and R. Simard, TestU01: A C Library for Empirical Testing of Random Number Generators, ACM TOMACS, 2007
hamming_independence.summary: The Hamming Independence test constructs a contingency table of weight categories for consecutive block pairs and applies a chi-squared test of independence.
hamming_independence.description:
This test (sstring_HammingIndep in TestU01) divides the bitstream into non-overlapping pairs of consecutive blocks. For each pair, the Hamming weights of both blocks are computed and categorized into bins. A contingency table is constructed and a chi-squared test of independence is applied.

Unlike the Hamming Correlation test (which only detects linear dependencies), this test can detect any form of dependence between consecutive block weights, including nonlinear relationships. The 5x5 contingency table with 16 degrees of freedom provides substantial power against a wide range of alternatives.
hamming_independence.mathematical_basis: Categorize each block weight into 5 bins. Build 5x5 contingency table. E[i][j] = rowTotal[i]*colTotal[j]/N. Chi-squared with df=16. P-value = igamc(8, chi2/2).

parameters.bitstream_count: Number of independent bitstreams to test (default: 1)
parameters.bitstream_length: Length of each bitstream in bits (minimum: 8192 bits)
parameters.block_size: Size of each block in bits (default: 32)

interpretation.pass: Block weight pairs are consistent with independence, no detectable dependence pattern.
interpretation.fail: Significant dependence detected between consecutive block weights, indicating structural flaws.

hamming_independence.recommendations: Use at least 1,000,000 bits. This test is more powerful than the Hamming Correlation test for detecting nonlinear dependencies.
example_applications: Detecting nonlinear dependencies in generator output
Testing for complex weight dependencies in consecutive words
Comprehensive independence testing for cryptographic generators

string_run.name: String Run Test
string_run.nist_reference: P. L'Ecuyer and R. Simard, TestU01: A C Library for Empirical Testing of Random Number Generators, ACM TOMACS, 2007
string_run.summary: The String Run test counts runs of consecutive identical bits and compares the distribution of run lengths against the expected geometric distribution.
string_run.description:
This test (sstring_Run in TestU01) counts runs of consecutive 0-bits and 1-bits directly in the bitstream. A run is a maximal sequence of identical consecutive bits. Under H0, the probability of a run of length k is (1/2)^k for k < maxLen and (1/2)^(maxLen-1) for k >= maxLen.

Separate chi-squared tests are applied to the 0-run and 1-run length distributions, then combined. This is distinct from the sknuth_Run test (which operates on uniform values) — it works directly on the raw bit string. This test is effective at detecting generators with abnormal bit repetition patterns.
string_run.mathematical_basis: Count 0-runs and 1-runs of lengths 1..maxLen-1 and >=maxLen. P(length=k) = (1/2)^k. Combined chi-squared = chi2_0 + chi2_1. df = 2*(maxLen-1). P-value = igamc(df/2, chi2/2).

parameters.bitstream_count: Number of independent bitstreams to test (default: 1)
parameters.bitstream_length: Length of each bitstream in bits (minimum: 8192 bits)

interpretation.pass: The distribution of bit-run lengths matches the expected geometric distribution.
interpretation.fail: Run lengths deviate significantly, indicating abnormal bit repetition or avoidance patterns.

string_run.recommendations: Use at least 1,000,000 bits. This test complements the NIST Runs test by examining the full distribution of run lengths rather than just the total count.
example_applications: Detecting abnormal bit repetition patterns
Testing for sticky bits or bit avoidance in generators
Evaluating hardware RNGs for bit-level independence

autocorrelation.name: Autocorrelation Test
autocorrelation.nist_reference: P. L'Ecuyer and R. Simard, TestU01: A C Library for Empirical Testing of Random Number Generators, ACM TOMACS, 2007
autocorrelation.summary: The Autocorrelation test computes the bit-level autocorrelation at a given lag and tests whether it deviates from zero.
autocorrelation.description:
This test (sstring_AutoCor in TestU01) computes the autocorrelation of the bitstream at lag d by XORing each bit with the bit d positions ahead. The sum of XOR values A should equal (n-d)/2 under the null hypothesis. The test standardizes this to a z-score and computes a two-sided p-value.

Unlike the Sample Correlation test which works on uniform values, this test operates directly on the raw bits. It detects generators whose output bits have dependencies at specific lag distances, which can indicate periodic structure or linear feedback patterns.
autocorrelation.mathematical_basis: A = sum(bit[i] XOR bit[i+d]) for i=0..n-d-1. E[A] = (n-d)/2. Var[A] = (n-d)/4. z = (A - E[A])/sqrt(Var[A]). P-value = erfc(|z|/sqrt(2)).

parameters.bitstream_count: Number of independent bitstreams to test (default: 1)
parameters.bitstream_length: Length of each bitstream in bits (minimum: 8192 bits)

interpretation.pass: No significant autocorrelation at the specified lag, consistent with bit-level independence.
interpretation.fail: Significant autocorrelation detected, indicating dependencies between bits at the given lag distance.

autocorrelation.recommendations: Test at multiple lag values (1, 2, 4, 8, etc.) for comprehensive coverage. Use at least 1,000,000 bits.
example_applications: Detecting periodic patterns in LFSR-based generators
Testing for bit-level serial dependencies
Evaluating hardware RNGs for temporal correlations

periods_in_strings.name: Periods in Strings Test
periods_in_strings.nist_reference: P. L'Ecuyer and R. Simard, TestU01: A C Library for Empirical Testing of Random Number Generators, ACM TOMACS, 2007
periods_in_strings.summary: The Periods in Strings test checks whether blocks of the bitstream exhibit periodic structure more or less often than expected by chance.
periods_in_strings.description:
This test (sstring_PeriodsInStrings in TestU01) divides the bitstream into non-overlapping blocks and determines whether each block has a periodic structure. A block of length L has period p if bit[i] = bit[i mod p] for all positions. Under randomness, periodic blocks are rare.

The test counts the number of periodic blocks and compares against the expected count using a normal approximation. It detects generators that produce repetitive patterns within output words, which can indicate underlying algebraic structure.
periods_in_strings.mathematical_basis: For each block of length L, find minimum period p < L. Count periodic blocks. Expected count based on probability of random periodicity. z-score from normal approximation. P-value = erfc(|z|/sqrt(2)).

parameters.bitstream_count: Number of independent bitstreams to test (default: 1)
parameters.bitstream_length: Length of each bitstream in bits (minimum: 8192 bits)
parameters.block_size: Size of each block in bits (default: 16)

interpretation.pass: The frequency of periodic blocks is consistent with randomness.
interpretation.fail: Too many or too few periodic blocks detected, indicating structural patterns.

periods_in_strings.recommendations: Use block sizes that are composite numbers (like 12, 16, 24) for best sensitivity, as they have more divisors and thus more opportunities for periodicity.
example_applications: Detecting repetitive patterns in generator output
Testing for algebraic structure in output words
Evaluating generators for periodic defects

longest_head_run.name: Longest Head Run Test
longest_head_run.nist_reference: P. L'Ecuyer and R. Simard, TestU01: A C Library for Empirical Testing of Random Number Generators, ACM TOMACS, 2007
longest_head_run.summary: The Longest Head Run test finds the longest run of consecutive 1-bits within each block and tests whether the distribution matches the expected Gumbel-like distribution.
longest_head_run.description:
This test (sstring_LongestHeadRun in TestU01) divides the bitstream into blocks and finds the longest run of consecutive 1-bits (head run) within each block. Under randomness, the expected longest run in a block of n bits is approximately log2(n).

The test bins the longest run lengths across multiple blocks and applies a chi-squared goodness-of-fit test against the theoretical distribution. This is similar to the NIST Longest Run Of Ones test but uses the TestU01 framework's statistical methodology.
longest_head_run.mathematical_basis: For each block, find max consecutive 1-run. P(longest >= k) ≈ 1 - exp(-n*2^(-k)). Bin runs around log2(blockSize). Chi-squared with df=5. P-value = igamc(5/2, chi2/2).

parameters.bitstream_count: Number of independent bitstreams to test (default: 1)
parameters.bitstream_length: Length of each bitstream in bits (minimum: 8192 bits)
parameters.block_size: Size of each block in bits (default: 1000)

interpretation.pass: The distribution of longest head runs matches the expected distribution.
interpretation.fail: Longest runs deviate from expectation, indicating clustering or avoidance of consecutive 1-bits.

longest_head_run.recommendations: Use block size of 1000 or more for meaningful results. Use at least 1,000,000 bits for sufficient blocks.
example_applications: Detecting clustering of 1-bits in generator output
Testing for abnormal run patterns
Evaluating generators for cryptographic applications

fourier_spectral.name: Fourier Spectral Test
fourier_spectral.nist_reference: P. L'Ecuyer and R. Simard, TestU01: A C Library for Empirical Testing of Random Number Generators, ACM TOMACS, 2007
fourier_spectral.summary: The Fourier Spectral test computes the discrete Fourier transform of the bitstream and tests whether spectral peak heights follow the expected distribution.
fourier_spectral.description:
This test (sspectral_Fourier3 in TestU01) maps the bitstream to +1/-1 values and computes the discrete Fourier transform. Under randomness, the squared magnitudes of the Fourier coefficients follow a chi-squared(2) distribution (i.e., exponential).

The test counts how many spectral peaks fall below a threshold T where 95% should fall below T under H0. A normal approximation tests whether the observed proportion matches the expected 95%. This test detects periodic or quasi-periodic patterns in the generator output that manifest as spectral peaks.
fourier_spectral.mathematical_basis: Map bits to X[i] = 2*bit[i]-1. DFT: M[k]^2 = |sum(X[i]*exp(-2pi*i*k/n))|^2. Threshold T^2 = 2n*ln(20). Count peaks below T. z = (N_below - 0.95*nFreq)/sqrt(0.95*0.05*nFreq). P-value = erfc(|z|/sqrt(2)).

parameters.bitstream_count: Number of independent bitstreams to test (default: 1)
parameters.bitstream_length: Length of each bitstream in bits (minimum: 8192 bits)

interpretation.pass: Spectral peak heights follow the expected distribution, no periodic structure detected.
interpretation.fail: Abnormal number of spectral peaks, indicating periodic or quasi-periodic patterns.

fourier_spectral.recommendations: This test is computationally intensive. Uses up to 8192 bits for the DFT computation for efficiency.
example_applications: Detecting periodic patterns in generator output
Testing for frequency-domain anomalies
Evaluating generators for spectral purity

entropy_discretization.name: Entropy Discretization Test
entropy_discretization.nist_reference: P. L'Ecuyer and R. Simard, TestU01: A C Library for Empirical Testing of Random Number Generators, ACM TOMACS, 2007
entropy_discretization.summary: The Entropy Discretization test measures the sample entropy of L-bit patterns and tests whether it is close to the maximum entropy.
entropy_discretization.description:
This test (sentrop_EntropyDisc in TestU01) divides the bitstream into non-overlapping blocks of L bits, treating each as an integer. It computes the sample entropy H = -sum(p_i * log2(p_i)) and tests whether H is close to the maximum entropy L.

Under randomness, all 2^L patterns are equally likely and the entropy should be approximately L bits. The test uses the Miller-Madow bias correction and a normal approximation to compute a z-score. Low entropy indicates non-uniform pattern distribution, suggesting generator weakness.
entropy_discretization.mathematical_basis: H = -sum(p_i * log2(p_i)). E[H] ≈ L - (2^L - 1)/(2*n*ln(2)) (Miller-Madow). Var[H] ≈ (2^L - 1)/(2*n^2*ln(2)^2). z = (H - E[H])/sqrt(Var[H]). P-value = erfc(|z|/sqrt(2)).

parameters.bitstream_count: Number of independent bitstreams to test (default: 1)
parameters.bitstream_length: Length of each bitstream in bits (minimum: 8192 bits)
parameters.block_size: Size of each pattern block in bits (default: 8)

interpretation.pass: The sample entropy is close to maximum, consistent with uniform pattern distribution.
interpretation.fail: Entropy significantly below maximum, indicating non-uniform pattern frequencies.

entropy_discretization.recommendations: Choose block size L such that the number of blocks is much larger than 2^L. For L=8, need at least 256*20 = 5120 bits. Use at least 1,000,000 bits.
example_applications: Measuring effective entropy of generator output
Detecting non-uniform pattern distributions
Evaluating entropy sources for cryptographic key generation

multinomial_bits_over.name: Multinomial Bits Over Test
multinomial_bits_over.nist_reference: P. L'Ecuyer and R. Simard, TestU01: A C Library for Empirical Testing of Random Number Generators, ACM TOMACS, 2007
multinomial_bits_over.summary: The Multinomial Bits Over test counts overlapping L-bit patterns and tests whether all patterns occur with equal frequency.
multinomial_bits_over.description:
This test (smultin_MultinomialBitsOver in TestU01) uses a sliding window to extract overlapping L-bit tuples from the bitstream. Each tuple is treated as an L-bit integer, and the frequency of each of the 2^L possible patterns is counted.

Under randomness, all patterns should appear with equal frequency. A chi-squared goodness-of-fit test with 2^L - 1 degrees of freedom compares observed counts against the expected uniform distribution. The overlapping approach provides more samples than non-overlapping methods, increasing statistical power.
multinomial_bits_over.mathematical_basis: Extract n-L+1 overlapping L-tuples. Expected count per pattern = (n-L+1)/2^L. Chi-squared = sum((O_i - E)^2/E). df = 2^L - 1. P-value = igamc(df/2, chi2/2).

parameters.bitstream_count: Number of independent bitstreams to test (default: 1)
parameters.bitstream_length: Length of each bitstream in bits (minimum: 8192 bits)
parameters.tuple_size: Size of each tuple in bits (default: 4)

interpretation.pass: All L-bit patterns occur with approximately equal frequency, consistent with randomness.
interpretation.fail: Significant non-uniformity in pattern frequencies detected.

multinomial_bits_over.recommendations: Choose tuple size L such that the bitstream has at least 5*2^L tuples. For L=4, need at least 80 bits. For L=10, need at least 5120 bits.
example_applications: Testing for non-uniform bit pattern distributions
Detecting overlapping pattern anomalies
Comprehensive multinomial testing of generator output

rank_32x32.name: Binary Rank 32x32 Test
rank_32x32.nist_reference: George Marsaglia, DIEHARD: A Battery of Tests of Randomness, 1996
rank_32x32.summary: Forms random 32x32 binary matrices and tests the distribution of matrix ranks using Gaussian elimination over GF(2).
rank_32x32.description:
Forms 32x32 binary matrices from 32 consecutive 32-bit random integers, determines the matrix rank using Gaussian elimination over GF(2), and performs a chi-squared test on the rank distribution. The expected rank probabilities are: P(rank=32)≈0.2888, P(rank=31)≈0.5776, P(rank=30)≈0.1284, P(rank≤29)≈0.0052.

From the original Dieharder suite: this test is considered reliable.
rank_32x32.mathematical_basis: A random binary matrix of size n×n has rank distribution determined by the probability of each row being linearly independent over GF(2). P(rank=n) = prod_{i=0}^{n-1}(1-2^{i-n}). Chi-squared with 3 df over 4 rank bins.

parameters.bitstream_count: Number of independent bitstreams to test (default: 1)
parameters.bitstream_length: Length of each bitstream in bits (minimum: 262144)

interpretation.pass: Matrix rank distribution matches expected probabilities.
interpretation.fail: Anomalous rank distribution detected.

rank_32x32.recommendations: Each matrix requires 1024 bits. Ensure sufficient bitstream length for statistical significance (at least 256 matrices).
example_applications: Testing linear independence of random bit vectors
Detecting linear dependencies in generator output

rank_6x8.name: Binary Rank 6x8 Test
rank_6x8.nist_reference: George Marsaglia, DIEHARD: A Battery of Tests of Randomness, 1996
rank_6x8.summary: Forms random 6x8 binary matrices and tests the distribution of matrix ranks.
rank_6x8.description:
Forms 6x8 binary matrices from 6 random bytes, determines the rank using Gaussian elimination over GF(2), and performs a chi-squared test on the rank distribution. Maximum possible rank is 6.

From the original Dieharder suite: this test is considered reliable.
rank_6x8.mathematical_basis: For a random 6x8 binary matrix, the rank distribution follows GF(2) linear algebra theory. Expected: P(rank=6)≈0.7731, P(rank=5)≈0.2028, P(rank=4)≈0.0239, P(rank≤3)≈0.0003. Chi-squared with 3 df.

parameters.bitstream_count: Number of independent bitstreams to test (default: 1)
parameters.bitstream_length: Length of each bitstream in bits (minimum: 262144)

interpretation.pass: Matrix rank distribution matches expected probabilities.
interpretation.fail: Anomalous rank distribution detected.

rank_6x8.recommendations: Each matrix requires 48 bits. Very efficient test — many matrices from modest bitstream lengths.
example_applications: Testing linear independence at byte level
Detecting rank deficiency patterns

bitstream.name: Bitstream Test
bitstream.nist_reference: George Marsaglia, DIEHARD: A Battery of Tests of Randomness, 1996
bitstream.summary: Counts missing 20-bit words in overlapping windows and tests against the expected normal distribution.
bitstream.description:
Treats the random bitstream as a sequence of overlapping 20-bit words. There are 2^20 = 1,048,576 possible 20-bit words. From a string of overlapping windows, the number of missing words is counted. The count should be approximately normally distributed with mean 141,909 and sigma 428.

From the original Dieharder suite: this test is considered reliable.
bitstream.mathematical_basis: With N overlapping 20-bit words from 2^20 possible values, the expected number of missing values follows from the occupancy problem: E[missing] ≈ 2^20 * (1 - 1/2^20)^N ≈ 141,909 for N=2^21. Z-test with sigma=428.

parameters.bitstream_count: Number of independent bitstreams to test (default: 1)
parameters.bitstream_length: Length of each bitstream in bits (minimum: 2097172)

interpretation.pass: Number of missing 20-bit words is consistent with random expectation.
interpretation.fail: Anomalous number of missing words detected.

bitstream.recommendations: Requires a large bitstream — at least 2^21 + 19 bits. Tests for completeness of 20-bit pattern coverage.
example_applications: Testing pattern coverage completeness
Detecting sparse occupancy anomalies

opso.name: Overlapping Pairs Sparse Occupancy (OPSO) Test
opso.nist_reference: George Marsaglia, DIEHARD: A Battery of Tests of Randomness, 1996
opso.summary: Tests sparse occupancy of 2-letter words from a 1024-letter alphabet (10-bit letters).
opso.description:
Generates 2-letter words from an alphabet of 1024 letters, where each letter is formed from 10 consecutive bits. Counts missing words. The number missing should be approximately normal with mean 141,909 and sigma 290.

WARNING: From the original Dieharder suite — this test is marked as suspect due to known issues with sample size relative to the word space.
opso.mathematical_basis: 1024^2 = 2^20 possible 2-letter words. Missing word count follows occupancy distribution with E[missing]=141909, sigma=290. Z-test.

parameters.bitstream_count: Number of independent bitstreams to test (default: 1)
parameters.bitstream_length: Length of each bitstream in bits (minimum: 262144)

interpretation.pass: Missing word count is consistent with random expectation.
interpretation.fail: Anomalous missing word count. NOTE: confirm with other tests — this test is marked suspect.

opso.recommendations: Use with caution. Results should be confirmed with other tests.
example_applications: Sparse occupancy testing
Pattern completeness analysis

oqso.name: Overlapping Quadruples Sparse Occupancy (OQSO) Test
oqso.nist_reference: George Marsaglia, DIEHARD: A Battery of Tests of Randomness, 1996
oqso.summary: Tests sparse occupancy of 4-letter words from a 32-letter alphabet (5-bit letters).
oqso.description:
Generates 4-letter words from an alphabet of 32 letters (5-bit). Counts missing words. Expected: mean 141,909, sigma 295.

WARNING: From the original Dieharder suite — this test is marked as suspect.
oqso.mathematical_basis: 32^4 = 2^20 possible 4-letter words. Occupancy analysis with E[missing]=141909, sigma=295.

parameters.bitstream_count: Number of independent bitstreams to test (default: 1)
parameters.bitstream_length: Length of each bitstream in bits (minimum: 262144)

interpretation.pass: Missing word count is consistent with random expectation.
interpretation.fail: Anomalous count. NOTE: confirm — test is suspect.

oqso.recommendations: Use with caution. Confirm failures with other tests.
example_applications: Sparse occupancy testing at 5-bit granularity

dna.name: DNA Test
dna.nist_reference: George Marsaglia, DIEHARD: A Battery of Tests of Randomness, 1996
dna.summary: Tests sparse occupancy of 10-letter words from a 4-letter DNA-like alphabet (2-bit letters).
dna.description:
Uses an alphabet of 4 letters (C,G,A,T) from 2-bit chunks. Forms 10-letter words (20 bits). Counts missing words. Expected: mean 141,909, sigma 339.

WARNING: From the original Dieharder suite — this test is marked as suspect.
dna.mathematical_basis: 4^10 = 2^20 possible 10-letter words. Occupancy analysis with E[missing]=141909, sigma=339.

parameters.bitstream_count: Number of independent bitstreams to test (default: 1)
parameters.bitstream_length: Length of each bitstream in bits (minimum: 262144)

interpretation.pass: Missing word count is consistent with random expectation.
interpretation.fail: Anomalous count. NOTE: confirm — test is suspect.

dna.recommendations: Use with caution. The DNA analogy tests 2-bit letter patterns.
example_applications: Testing 2-bit pattern distributions
DNA-sequence-style randomness analysis

count_1s_stream.name: Count the 1s (Stream) Test
count_1s_stream.nist_reference: George Marsaglia, DIEHARD: A Battery of Tests of Randomness, 1996
count_1s_stream.summary: Counts 1-bits in successive bytes, converts to letters, and tests the distribution of 5-letter words.
count_1s_stream.description:
Counts the number of 1-bits in each successive byte, converts counts to letters (0-2→A, 3→B, 4→C, 5→D, 6-8→E), forms overlapping 5-letter words, and tests word frequencies against the expected multinomial distribution.

From the original Dieharder suite: this test is considered reliable.
count_1s_stream.mathematical_basis: Letter probabilities from binomial(8,0.5): P(A)=37/256, P(B)=56/256, P(C)=70/256, P(D)=56/256, P(E)=37/256. Chi-squared over 5^5=3125 possible words with df=3124.

parameters.bitstream_count: Number of independent bitstreams to test (default: 1)
parameters.bitstream_length: Length of each bitstream in bits (minimum: 262144)

interpretation.pass: 5-letter word frequencies match expected multinomial distribution.
interpretation.fail: Non-uniform word distribution detected.

count_1s_stream.recommendations: Tests byte-level bit count patterns. Requires sufficient data for 3125 categories.
example_applications: Testing byte-level popcount distributions
Detecting bit-count correlation patterns

count_1s_byte.name: Count the 1s (Byte) Test
count_1s_byte.nist_reference: George Marsaglia, DIEHARD: A Battery of Tests of Randomness, 1996
count_1s_byte.summary: Like Count 1s Stream but selects specific bytes from 32-bit integers.
count_1s_byte.description:
Similar to Count 1s Stream but extracts specific bytes from each 32-bit integer rather than using successive bytes. Tests the resulting 5-letter word distribution.

From the original Dieharder suite: this test is considered reliable.
count_1s_byte.mathematical_basis: Same letter probabilities as stream version. Chi-squared over 3125 categories.

parameters.bitstream_count: Number of independent bitstreams to test (default: 1)
parameters.bitstream_length: Length of each bitstream in bits (minimum: 262144)

interpretation.pass: Word frequencies match expected distribution.
interpretation.fail: Non-uniform distribution detected.

count_1s_byte.recommendations: Complements the stream version by testing byte-selection patterns.
example_applications: Testing byte-selection independence
Detecting intra-word bit-count bias

3dsphere.name: 3D Spheres Test
3dsphere.nist_reference: George Marsaglia, DIEHARD: A Battery of Tests of Randomness, 1996
3dsphere.summary: Places random points in a 3D cube and tests whether the minimum pairwise distance follows the expected exponential distribution.
3dsphere.description:
Places N random points in a cube of edge 1000. For each pair, finds the Euclidean distance. The cube of the minimum distance should be approximately exponentially distributed.

From the original Dieharder suite: this test is considered reliable.
3dsphere.mathematical_basis: For N points in a cube of volume V, P(D_min^3 < x) ≈ 1 - exp(-lambda*x) where lambda = N*(N-1)/2 * (4*pi/3) / V.

parameters.bitstream_count: Number of independent bitstreams to test (default: 1)
parameters.bitstream_length: Length of each bitstream in bits (minimum: 524288)

interpretation.pass: Minimum distance distribution consistent with random point placement.
interpretation.fail: Anomalous clustering or spacing in 3D.

3dsphere.recommendations: O(N^2) computation limits N to ~50000. Default 4000 is effective.
example_applications: 3D spatial uniformity testing
Monte Carlo point placement validation

sums.name: Overlapping Sums Test
sums.nist_reference: George Marsaglia, DIEHARD: A Battery of Tests of Randomness, 1996
sums.summary: Tests overlapping sums of 100 consecutive random floats using a KS test.
sums.description:
Generates random floats on [0,1) and forms overlapping sums of 100 consecutive values. The sums are standardized and tested via Kolmogorov-Smirnov.

WARNING: From the original Dieharder suite — this test is DEPRECATED and marked as DO NOT USE. It produces systematically non-flat p-value distributions for ALL generators, including cryptographically secure ones. Included only for completeness of the Diehard battery.
sums.mathematical_basis: E[sum of 100 uniform] = 50, Var = 100/12. Standardize and apply KS test.

parameters.bitstream_count: Number of independent bitstreams to test (default: 1)
parameters.bitstream_length: Length of each bitstream in bits (minimum: 262144)

interpretation.pass: Sums consistent with normal distribution.
interpretation.fail: DO NOT TRUST — this test is known to produce false results for all generators.

sums.recommendations: DO NOT USE for production testing. Included only for battery completeness. Use rgb_lagged_sum or other tests instead.
example_applications: Historical reference only — not recommended for actual testing

runs.name: Runs Test
runs.nist_reference: George Marsaglia, DIEHARD: A Battery of Tests of Randomness, 1996
runs.summary: Counts ascending runs of various lengths in a sequence of random floats and tests the run length distribution.
runs.description:
Generates random floats on [0,1) and counts ascending runs (maximal increasing subsequences). The distribution of run lengths is compared against the theoretical distribution using chi-squared.

From the original Dieharder suite: this test is considered reliable.
runs.mathematical_basis: P(ascending run of length k) = k/(k+1)! for k≥1. Bins: length 1 through 5, and ≥6. Chi-squared with df=5.

parameters.bitstream_count: Number of independent bitstreams to test (default: 1)
parameters.bitstream_length: Length of each bitstream in bits (minimum: 262144)

interpretation.pass: Run length distribution matches theoretical expectation.
interpretation.fail: Anomalous run length distribution detected.

runs.recommendations: Tests for monotonicity patterns in the random sequence.
example_applications: Detecting monotonic bias
Testing sequential independence

craps.name: Craps Test
craps.nist_reference: George Marsaglia, DIEHARD: A Battery of Tests of Randomness, 1996
craps.summary: Plays many games of craps and tests whether the win rate matches the theoretical probability of 244/495.
craps.description:
Simulates many games of craps using random numbers. The number of wins should follow a binomial distribution with p=244/495≈0.4929. A z-test compares the observed win count to the expected value.

From the original Dieharder suite: this test is considered reliable.
craps.mathematical_basis: Win probability p=244/495. E[wins]=N*p, Var=N*p*(1-p). Z-test: z=(wins-E)/sqrt(Var).

parameters.bitstream_count: Number of independent bitstreams to test (default: 1)
parameters.bitstream_length: Length of each bitstream in bits (minimum: 1048576)
parameters.num_games: Number of craps games to simulate (default: 200000, range: 1000-1000000)

interpretation.pass: Win rate is consistent with the theoretical probability.
interpretation.fail: Anomalous win rate detected.

craps.recommendations: Requires substantial data — each game uses ~64+ bits per dice roll. Default 200000 games is effective.
example_applications: Testing random number generators for gaming applications
Validating dice simulation fairness

marsaglia_tsang_gcd.name: Marsaglia-Tsang GCD Test
marsaglia_tsang_gcd.nist_reference: G. Marsaglia, W.W. Tsang, Some Difficult-to-pass Tests of Randomness, 2002
marsaglia_tsang_gcd.summary: Computes GCDs of random integer pairs and tests the distribution of GCD values against number-theoretic expectations.
marsaglia_tsang_gcd.description:
Generates pairs of random 32-bit integers, computes their GCD using the Euclidean algorithm, and tests the distribution of GCD values. The probability that gcd(u,v)=d is 6/(pi^2*d^2) for random integers. A chi-squared test compares observed vs expected GCD value frequencies.

From the original Dieharder suite: this test is considered reliable. Many linear congruential generators fail this test.
marsaglia_tsang_gcd.mathematical_basis: P(gcd=d) = 6/(pi^2*d^2). P(coprime)=6/pi^2≈0.6079. Chi-squared over GCD value bins with df=6.

parameters.bitstream_count: Number of independent bitstreams to test (default: 1)
parameters.bitstream_length: Length of each bitstream in bits (minimum: 524288)

interpretation.pass: GCD distribution matches number-theoretic expectations.
interpretation.fail: Anomalous GCD distribution — often indicates linear congruential generator weaknesses.

marsaglia_tsang_gcd.recommendations: Very effective at detecting LCG weaknesses. Default 10000 pairs is sufficient. Each pair needs 64 bits.
example_applications: Detecting LCG weaknesses
Testing coprimality distribution
Number-theoretic randomness validation

dab_bytedistrib.name: DAB Byte Distribution Test
dab_bytedistrib.summary: Tests the distribution of all 256 possible byte values in the random bitstream for uniformity using a chi-squared goodness-of-fit test.
dab_bytedistrib.description:
The DAB Byte Distribution test examines the frequency of occurrence of each possible byte value (0-255) in the random bitstream. For a truly random source, all 256 byte values should occur with approximately equal frequency.

The bitstream is divided into consecutive bytes and the occurrence count for each of the 256 possible values is tallied. A chi-squared goodness-of-fit test with 255 degrees of freedom compares the observed distribution against the expected uniform distribution.

From the original Dieharder suite: this test is considered reliable and effective for detecting byte-level bias in random number generators.
dab_bytedistrib.mathematical_basis: Given N bytes, the expected count for each byte value is E = N/256. The chi-squared statistic is X^2 = sum_{i=0}^{255} (O_i - E)^2 / E, with 255 degrees of freedom. The p-value is computed from the upper tail of the chi-squared distribution.

parameters.bitstream_count: Number of independent bitstreams to test (default: 1)
parameters.bitstream_length: Length of each bitstream in bits (minimum: 524288 bits = 64KB)

interpretation.pass: All 256 byte values occur with approximately equal frequency, consistent with randomness.
interpretation.fail: Significant non-uniformity in byte value frequencies detected, indicating potential bias.

dab_bytedistrib.recommendations: Requires a large sample size for good statistics — at least 64KB (524,288 bits). More data yields better sensitivity. This test complements bit-level tests by checking for byte-level patterns.
example_applications: Detecting byte-level bias in hardware random number generators
Validating stream cipher output byte distribution
Quality assurance for cryptographic key generation

dab_dct.name: DAB Discrete Cosine Transform Test
dab_dct.summary: Applies a Discrete Cosine Transform to blocks of random values and tests the frequency-domain coefficients for anomalies.
dab_dct.description:
The DAB DCT test converts blocks of random numbers into the frequency domain using the Discrete Cosine Transform (Type II). For truly random input uniformly distributed on [0,1), the DCT coefficients (excluding the DC component) should be approximately normally distributed with known mean and variance.

The test processes the bitstream in blocks of N values (default 256), computes the DCT-II for each block, standardizes the non-DC coefficients, and accumulates a chi-squared statistic from the sum of squared standardized coefficients.

From the original Dieharder suite: this test is rated as the second most effective test in the entire Dieharder battery for detecting random number generator weaknesses, after dab_monobit2.
dab_dct.mathematical_basis: For each block of N uniform [0,1) values, the DCT-II coefficient Y[k] for k>=1 has E[Y[k]]=0 and Var[Y[k]]=N/24. The standardized statistic z[k] = Y[k] / sqrt(N/24) should be standard normal. The sum of squared z-values follows a chi-squared distribution with df = numBlocks * (N-1).

parameters.bitstream_count: Number of independent bitstreams to test (default: 1)
parameters.bitstream_length: Length of each bitstream in bits (minimum: 262144 bits)
parameters.block_size: Number of values per DCT block (default: 256, range: 64-4096)

interpretation.pass: DCT coefficients follow the expected distribution for random input.
interpretation.fail: Anomalous frequency-domain structure detected, indicating non-random patterns.

dab_dct.recommendations: The default block size of 256 provides good sensitivity. Larger blocks detect subtler patterns but require more data. This test is particularly effective at detecting linear congruential generator weaknesses.
example_applications: Detecting periodic or structured patterns in generator output
Frequency-domain analysis of random number quality
Validating generators used in Monte Carlo simulations

dab_filltree.name: DAB Fill Tree Test
dab_filltree.summary: Tests binary tree filling patterns using random bits to detect non-random traversal behavior.
dab_filltree.description:
The DAB Fill Tree test constructs a complete binary tree of a specified depth and uses random bits to perform walks from the root to leaves. At each internal node, a random bit determines whether to go left (0) or right (1). After many walks, the distribution of leaf visit counts is examined.

For truly random walks, each leaf at depth D should be visited approximately N/2^D times, where N is the number of walks. The observed distribution of visit counts is compared to the expected Poisson distribution using a chi-squared test.

WARNING: From the original Dieharder suite — this test is marked as potentially unreliable. Results should be interpreted with caution, and failures should be confirmed with other tests before concluding non-randomness.
dab_filltree.mathematical_basis: With 2^D leaves and N walks, each leaf has expected visits lambda = N/2^D following approximately Poisson(lambda). The observed frequency of k-visit leaves is compared to N_leaves * P(X=k) where P(X=k) = lambda^k * e^(-lambda) / k!, using a chi-squared test.

parameters.bitstream_count: Number of independent bitstreams to test (default: 1)
parameters.bitstream_length: Length of each bitstream in bits (minimum: 262144 bits)
parameters.tree_depth: Depth of the binary tree (default: 15, range: 5-32)

interpretation.pass: Tree filling patterns are consistent with random bit generation.
interpretation.fail: Non-random tree traversal patterns detected. NOTE: confirm with other tests due to this test's reliability concerns.

dab_filltree.recommendations: Use with caution — this test may produce unreliable results according to the original Dieharder documentation. Always confirm failures using dab_filltree2 or other tests. Tree depth of 15 is recommended; deeper trees require exponentially more data.
example_applications: Testing random bit generators for tree-traversal applications
Detecting correlations in sequential bit output
Research-level randomness analysis

dab_filltree2.name: DAB Fill Tree 2 Test
dab_filltree2.summary: A refined variant of the Fill Tree test with improved methodology for testing binary tree filling patterns.
dab_filltree2.description:
The DAB Fill Tree 2 test is an improved version of the original Fill Tree test. It uses random bits to walk a complete binary tree and tracks internal node completion — specifically which nodes have had both their left and right subtrees visited.

The test performs many random walks from root to leaf, using Poisson-binned leaf visit counts for the chi-squared statistic. This refined approach corrects methodological issues in the original Fill Tree test, providing more reliable p-values.

From the original Dieharder suite: this test is considered reliable, improving significantly on the original Fill Tree test.
dab_filltree2.mathematical_basis: With 2^D leaves and N walks, leaf visit counts follow Poisson(lambda) where lambda = N/2^D. Visit counts are binned by frequency (0 visits, 1 visit, 2 visits, ...) and compared to the Poisson PMF. Chi-squared statistic is computed over bins with expected count >= 5.

parameters.bitstream_count: Number of independent bitstreams to test (default: 1)
parameters.bitstream_length: Length of each bitstream in bits (minimum: 262144 bits)
parameters.tree_depth: Depth of the binary tree (default: 15, range: 5-32)

interpretation.pass: Tree filling patterns and node completion rates are consistent with random bit generation.
interpretation.fail: Non-random tree filling patterns detected.

dab_filltree2.recommendations: Preferred over dab_filltree due to improved methodology. Tree depth of 15 is the standard setting. Ensure sufficient bitstream length — deeper trees require exponentially more data.
example_applications: Testing random bit generators for tree-traversal applications
Detecting correlations in sequential bit output
Replacement for dab_filltree with improved reliability

dab_monobit2.name: DAB Enhanced Monobit Test
dab_monobit2.summary: An enhanced monobit test that detects localized bit bias within non-overlapping blocks of the bitstream.
dab_monobit2.description:
The DAB Monobit 2 test extends the simple monobit (frequency) test by examining bit balance within individual non-overlapping blocks rather than globally. While a generator might have good overall 0/1 balance, it could still exhibit localized bias within smaller blocks.

The bitstream is divided into non-overlapping blocks of specified size. For each block, the number of 1-bits is counted and compared to the expected value of blockSize/2. The per-block deviations are accumulated into a chi-squared statistic.

From the original Dieharder suite: this test is rated as the single most effective test in the entire Dieharder battery for detecting random number generator weaknesses. It surpasses all other tests in sensitivity.
dab_monobit2.mathematical_basis: For each block of B bits, the number of 1s S_i has E[S_i] = B/2 and Var[S_i] = B/4 under randomness. The test statistic is X^2 = sum_{i=1}^{K} (S_i - B/2)^2 / (B/4), which follows a chi-squared distribution with K degrees of freedom, where K = floor(n/B) is the number of blocks.

parameters.bitstream_count: Number of independent bitstreams to test (default: 1)
parameters.bitstream_length: Length of each bitstream in bits (minimum: 1048576 bits = 128KB)
parameters.block_size: Size of each non-overlapping block in bits (default: 4096, range: 256-1048576)

interpretation.pass: Per-block bit balance is consistent with randomness across all blocks.
interpretation.fail: Localized bit bias detected in one or more blocks.

dab_monobit2.recommendations: This is the most powerful single test in Dieharder — run it as a priority. Use a large bitstream (128KB+) for best results. The default block size of 4096 bits is effective; smaller blocks increase sensitivity to local bias but require more total data.
example_applications: Primary randomness screening for random number generators
Detecting localized bias that global monobit tests miss
Cryptographic random number generator validation
Quality assurance for hardware true random number generators

rgb_bitdist.name: RGB Bit Distribution Test
rgb_bitdist.nist_reference: Robert G. Brown, Dieharder: A Random Number Test Suite, Duke University
rgb_bitdist.summary: Tests the distribution of overlapping n-bit patterns in the random bitstream for uniformity using a chi-squared goodness-of-fit test.
rgb_bitdist.description:
The RGB Bit Distribution test examines the frequency of all possible n-bit overlapping patterns in the random bitstream. For truly random data, all 2^n possible patterns should occur with approximately equal frequency.

At ntuple=1, this reduces to a monobit (frequency) test. At ntuple=2, it tests pair balance. Higher ntuple values test increasingly complex pattern distributions, making this a powerful generalization of simpler bit-level tests.

A sliding window of n bits is moved across the entire bitstream, counting each pattern's occurrences. A chi-squared test with 2^n - 1 degrees of freedom compares observed vs expected uniform frequencies.

From the original Dieharder suite: this test is considered reliable and very thorough, though computational cost increases with ntuple size.
rgb_bitdist.mathematical_basis: Given a bitstream of length L bits with overlapping windows of n bits, there are L-n+1 windows. Each of the 2^n patterns has expected count E = (L-n+1)/2^n. The chi-squared statistic is X^2 = sum_{j=0}^{2^n-1} (O_j - E)^2/E with df = 2^n - 1.

parameters.bitstream_count: Number of independent bitstreams to test (default: 1)
parameters.bitstream_length: Length of each bitstream in bits (minimum: 262144 bits)
parameters.ntuple: Size of bit patterns to test (default: 8, range: 1-12)

interpretation.pass: All n-bit patterns occur with approximately equal frequency, consistent with randomness.
interpretation.fail: Significant non-uniformity in n-bit pattern frequencies detected.

rgb_bitdist.recommendations: Start with small ntuple values (1-4) for quick screening, then increase for deeper analysis. Ntuple=8 is a good default. Ntuple values above 12 require very large bitstreams (at least 5 * 2^n bits recommended).
example_applications: Comprehensive bit-pattern uniformity testing
Generalized monobit analysis at multiple resolutions
Detecting subtle pattern biases in generator output

rgb_minimum_distance.name: RGB Minimum Distance Test
rgb_minimum_distance.nist_reference: Robert G. Brown, Dieharder: A Random Number Test Suite, Duke University
rgb_minimum_distance.summary: Places random points in a d-dimensional unit hypercube and tests whether the minimum pairwise distance follows the expected exponential distribution.
rgb_minimum_distance.description:
The RGB Minimum Distance test is a generalization of the original Diehard 2D sphere and 3D sphere tests, based on work by M. Fischler. It places N random points in a d-dimensional unit hypercube and finds the minimum Euclidean distance between any pair of points.

For truly random points, the d-th power of the minimum distance should be approximately exponentially distributed. The test computes the p-value from this exponential model, incorporating correction terms for the number of point pairs and the volume of the d-dimensional ball.

From the original Dieharder suite: this test is considered reliable and includes essential correction terms that the original Diehard 2D/3D tests lacked.
rgb_minimum_distance.mathematical_basis: For N uniform random points in [0,1)^d, the minimum distance D_min satisfies P(D_min^d < x) ≈ 1 - exp(-lambda * x), where lambda = N*(N-1)/2 * V_d and V_d = pi^(d/2) / Gamma(d/2+1) is the volume of the unit d-ball.

parameters.bitstream_count: Number of independent bitstreams to test (default: 1)
parameters.bitstream_length: Length of each bitstream in bits (minimum: 262144 bits)
parameters.num_points: Number of random points to place (default: 4000, range: 100-50000)

interpretation.pass: Minimum distance distribution is consistent with truly random point placement.
interpretation.fail: Anomalous clustering or spacing detected in random point placement.

rgb_minimum_distance.recommendations: Dimension 2 requires the least data. Higher dimensions require more points for reliable results. The O(N^2) pairwise computation limits practical N to ~50,000. Default of 4000 points in 2D is effective.
example_applications: Testing spatial uniformity of random point generators
Detecting clustering artifacts in Monte Carlo simulations
Validating random number generators for spatial applications

rgb_permutations.name: RGB Permutations Test
rgb_permutations.nist_reference: Robert G. Brown, Dieharder: A Random Number Test Suite, Duke University
rgb_permutations.summary: Tests whether orderings of successive non-overlapping groups of random numbers occur with equal probability among all possible permutations.
rgb_permutations.description:
The RGB Permutations test divides the random stream into non-overlapping groups of t consecutive values and determines the ordering (permutation) of each group. For truly random values, each of the t! possible orderings should occur with equal probability.

Unlike the original Diehard overlapping permutations test (operm5), this version uses non-overlapping windows, avoiding the covariance problems that made operm5 unreliable. The permutation rank is computed using the Lehmer code (factorial number system).

From the original Dieharder suite: this test is considered reliable and avoids the known issues with the overlapping version (diehard_operm5).
rgb_permutations.mathematical_basis: For groups of size t, there are t! possible permutations. With K non-overlapping groups, each permutation has expected count E = K/t!. The chi-squared statistic is X^2 = sum_{j=0}^{t!-1} (O_j - E)^2/E with df = t! - 1. The p-value is from the upper tail of the chi-squared distribution.

parameters.bitstream_count: Number of independent bitstreams to test (default: 1)
parameters.bitstream_length: Length of each bitstream in bits (minimum: 262144 bits)

interpretation.pass: Permutation frequencies are consistent with random ordering.
interpretation.fail: Non-uniform permutation distribution detected, indicating ordering bias.

rgb_permutations.recommendations: Tuple size 5 (120 permutations) is the standard. Larger values increase sensitivity but require exponentially more data (need at least 5*t! groups). Tuple size 10 requires 18 million+ groups. Preferred over diehard_operm5.
example_applications: Detecting ordering bias in random number sequences
Validating shuffle algorithms
Testing independence of consecutive random values

rgb_lagged_sum.name: RGB Lagged Sum Test
rgb_lagged_sum.nist_reference: Robert G. Brown, Dieharder: A Random Number Test Suite, Duke University
rgb_lagged_sum.summary: Tests for autocorrelation at various lag distances by summing uniformly distributed values sampled at regular intervals from the random stream.
rgb_lagged_sum.description:
The RGB Lagged Sum test examines bit-level autocorrelation at various lag distances. It sums uniform [0,1) values sampled from the random stream, taking every (lag+1)-th value and skipping the rest. The mean and variance of the resulting sum should match the expected values for independent uniform variates.

For each lag value, a z-score is computed comparing the observed sum to the expected value, and a two-sided p-value is derived from the normal distribution.

WARNING: From the original Dieharder suite — this test is marked as suspect and is known to produce false positives. Results should be interpreted with caution and confirmed with other tests before concluding non-randomness.
rgb_lagged_sum.mathematical_basis: For N uniform [0,1) values: E[sum] = N/2, Var[sum] = N/12. With stride = lag+1, N = floor(totalValues/stride). The z-score is z = (sum - N/2) / sqrt(N/12). The two-sided p-value is erfc(|z|/sqrt(2)).

parameters.bitstream_count: Number of independent bitstreams to test (default: 1)
parameters.bitstream_length: Length of each bitstream in bits (minimum: 262144 bits)
parameters.lag: Number of values to skip between samples (default: 0, range: 0-32)

interpretation.pass: Lagged sums are consistent with independent uniform random values.
interpretation.fail: Anomalous lagged sum detected. NOTE: confirm with other tests due to this test's known false positive issues.

rgb_lagged_sum.recommendations: Use with caution — this test is known to produce false positives according to the original Dieharder documentation. Always confirm failures with other tests. Lag=0 tests basic sum properties; higher lags test for autocorrelation at specific distances.
example_applications: Detecting autocorrelation in random number generators
Testing independence at various lag distances
Screening for lagged dependencies in generator output

rgb_kstest.name: RGB Kolmogorov-Smirnov Test
rgb_kstest.nist_reference: Robert G. Brown, Dieharder: A Random Number Test Suite, Duke University
rgb_kstest.summary: Applies the Kolmogorov-Smirnov goodness-of-fit test to verify that random values follow a uniform distribution on [0,1).
rgb_kstest.description:
The RGB KS Test applies the classical Kolmogorov-Smirnov goodness-of-fit test directly to uniform random values derived from the bitstream. It computes the maximum deviation between the empirical cumulative distribution function (CDF) of the observed values and the theoretical uniform CDF.

The test converts 32-bit integers from the bitstream into uniform [0,1) doubles, sorts them, and computes the KS statistic D = max(D+, D-) where D+ and D- measure the maximum upward and downward deviations of the empirical CDF from the theoretical uniform CDF.

From the original Dieharder suite: this test is considered reliable and is used internally by Dieharder to validate p-value distributions from other tests.
rgb_kstest.mathematical_basis: For N sorted uniform samples x_1 <= x_2 <= ... <= x_N: D+ = max_i(i/N - x_i), D- = max_i(x_i - (i-1)/N), D = max(D+, D-). The p-value uses the asymptotic formula with lambda = (sqrt(N) + 0.12 + 0.11/sqrt(N)) * D.

parameters.bitstream_count: Number of independent bitstreams to test (default: 1)
parameters.bitstream_length: Length of each bitstream in bits (minimum: 262144 bits)

interpretation.pass: Values are consistent with a uniform distribution on [0,1).
interpretation.fail: Significant deviation from uniform distribution detected.

rgb_kstest.recommendations: More data yields a more sensitive test. The KS test is non-parametric and does not require binning, making it complementary to chi-squared based tests. Best used as a fundamental uniformity check.
example_applications: Fundamental uniformity validation of random number generators
Meta-testing p-value distributions from other statistical tests
Non-parametric distribution testing without binning artifacts
